text: The surgeon identifies a tumor in the transverse colon and begins a transverse colectomy. A hanging maneuver is performed to elevate the transverse colon for resection.

The surgeon approaches the pedicle in the medial colic area, with the pancreas visible on both the left and right sides. The lesser sac is entered, and dissection begins from the root of the mesentery to remove the lymphovascular tissue along with the tumor. Energy devices are used for a suture-less transverse colectomy, with the lymphovascular pedicle taken from below and behind the pancreas.

The lesser sac is opened, and the left-sided colon is skeletonized. The surgeon takes the margin on the right side and creates space on the upper part of the transverse colon. A stapler is fired on both the left and right sides to complete the transverse colectomy. The pedicle is mobilized further.

The surgeon attempts to anastomose the two ends of the colon using a stapler but finds it challenging. The omentum is removed from the left side, followed by the right side, to facilitate mobilization. The hepatic flexure of the colon is dismantled and the right colon is mobilized two-thirds from above downwards to achieve a tension-free colocolic anastomosis.

The surgeon gradually dissects and mobilizes the duodenum and hepatic flexure, revealing the anatomical plane anterior to the fascia of Gerota. The right colon is properly mobilized, and attention shifts to the left side where the colon is carefully separated from the omentum.

The surgeon dismantles the splenic flexure, starting from a medial to lateral approach. The proximal one-third of the left colon is mobilized, facilitating the complete removal of the transverse colon. Both the right and left flexures are brought down for further surgical manipulation.

The surgeon performs an isoperistaltic side-to-side right-left colocolostomy using a blue cartridge from the patient's left side port. The staple line is reinforced with a crotch stitch to alleviate tension, followed by additional stitches to ensure the staple hole remains unobstructed. The procedure concludes with rapid extramucosal continuous suturing along the axis.
The surgical specimen is removed from the abdominal cavity using a fan retractor, positioned to facilitate extraction.
